claim_under_attack: The organizational logic for separation is real, but the benefits can be captured internally without imposing a two-company experience on customers.
evidence_or_reason: Side B asserts that internal separation can deliver the same organizational benefits as a formal split, but provides zero evidence that Netflix's existing management structure can enforce genuine P&L discipline, separate product roadmaps, and distinct capital allocation without external structural commitment. The artifact's own open questions acknowledge uncertainty about whether 'Netflix can implement internal business-unit separation with clean accountability and budgeting without a customer-facing brand split.' This is not a minor detail — it is the load-bearing mechanism of the entire recommendation. If internal separation fails to create real operating independence, the recommendation collapses into preserving the exact bundled-decision-making drag that motivated the split in the first place. The case packet notes that 'product decisions for streaming are constrained by DVD logistics,' and Side B offers no concrete governance design, precedent, or organizational mechanism to show this constraint would actually be relieved by an internal-only restructuring. The claim assumes the hardest part of the problem away.
